summary: My great-great grandfather, Peter August Adolphson, made a challenging journey from Sweden to the United States in 1886 when he was 18. He started with no English and little money but learned the language, ran a shoe store in St. Peter, Minnesota, and raised a family with his wife Anna. Peter worked hard through the Great Depression, eventually owning Adolphson Shoes, which he operated with his son Albin. His story embodies the immigrant pursuit of the American dream and left a lasting legacy in our family and community.

Peter August Adolphson was born and raised in a small rural community in Smaland, Sweden, where his family was relatively poor. Finding limited work opportunities there in 1886, Peter and his family decided it was best for him to emigrate to America. His father ran a small shoe shop in Sweden and taught Peter the trade, which proved invaluable after he moved. Though Peter left Sweden with a heavy heart, knowing travel was slow and expensive, his roots there shaped his early life and skills.

Peter August Adolphson arrived in the United States in the late 19th century after traveling from Sweden through England and Quebec to Chicago. He adapted to American life by changing the spelling of his last name to Adolphson and eventually settled in St. Peter, Minnesota. In the U.S., he built a successful shoe business and raised a family, embodying the immigrant experience and the American dream. His story highlights the challenges and opportunities faced by newcomers during that era.

Frank Holmberg was my great-great grandfather Peter August Adolphson's uncle who had already settled in America when Peter decided to emigrate. Frank helped Peter by providing a prepaid ticket for his journey from Sweden to the United States. This support was crucial in enabling Peter to make the long and difficult trip across the Atlantic in the late 1800s. Frank's role was a key part of Peter's successful immigration story.

England was a stopover point on Peter August Adolphson's journey from Sweden to the United States. After leaving Sweden, Peter traveled by ship to England before continuing on to Quebec and then Chicago. This leg of the journey was part of the long and expensive travel process immigrants faced in the late 1800s. England was one of the many places Peter passed through on his way to a new life in America.

Quebec was another important stop on Peter August Adolphson's immigration route from Sweden to the United States. After traveling from Sweden to England, Peter continued by ship to Quebec before finally reaching Chicago. This journey was slow and costly, reflecting the challenges faced by immigrants in the late 1800s. Quebec served as a gateway to North America for many immigrants like Peter.

Chicago was the first American city Peter August Adolphson arrived in after his long journey from Sweden through England and Quebec. He spent some time there before hearing about the Swedish immigrant communities in southern Minnesota. Chicago was a major hub for immigrants at the time, but Peter eventually moved on to settle in St. Peter, Minnesota. His time in Chicago marked the beginning of his new life in the United States.

Minnesota became home to Peter August Adolphson after he left Chicago, as he heard many Swedish immigrants were settling there. He moved to southern Minnesota and ultimately settled in the small town of St. Peter. Minnesota offered Peter the opportunity to build a life, work in the shoe business, and raise a family. His siblings also joined him there, creating a strong family presence in the region.

St. Peter, Minnesota, was where Peter August Adolphson finally settled after immigrating to the United States. There, he worked for 43 years in a small shoe store before becoming its owner during the Great Depression in 1933. The town was home to many Swedish immigrants, including Peter's siblings who later joined him. St. Peter became the center of Peter's family life and business legacy, and it remains important to our family history today.

The Great Depression was a challenging time when Peter August Adolphson took ownership of the shoe store in St. Peter in 1933. Despite the economic hardships of that era, Peter managed to run the business successfully with the help of his son Albin. This period tested Peter's patience and work ethic, but he persevered and kept the family business alive. His resilience during the Great Depression is a testament to his character and determination.

Albin Adolphson was Peter August Adolphson's son who helped run the family shoe store in St. Peter. He worked alongside his father during the Great Depression and later became co-owner of Adolphson Shoes after Peter passed away in 1943. Albin continued the family legacy by managing the business with Peter's brother Andrew until the store closed in 1965. Albin played a vital role in sustaining the family business through difficult times.

Andrew Adolphson was Peter August Adolphson's brother who joined him in settling in southern Minnesota. Andrew worked as an employee at the family shoe store and later became co-owner alongside Peter's son Albin after Peter died in 1943. Together, they operated Adolphson Shoes until it closed in 1965. Andrew's involvement helped keep the family business thriving for many years.

Esther Adolphson was one of Peter August Adolphson's sisters who joined him in settling in southern Minnesota after he had established himself there. Along with her siblings Andrew and Jenny, Esther became part of the family community in St. Peter. Their reunion in America helped strengthen the family ties far from their home in Sweden. Esther's move was part of the broader family migration story.

Jenny Adolphson was another sister of Peter August Adolphson who came to join him in southern Minnesota. Like her siblings Andrew and Esther, Jenny helped build the family presence in the area after emigrating from Sweden. Their reunion in Minnesota was important for maintaining family connections in a new country. Jenny's story is part of the larger narrative of the Adolphson family's immigration and settlement.

Anna Adolphson was the wife of Peter August Adolphson, and they were happily married for 51 years. Together, they raised four children in St. Peter, Minnesota, supporting Peter's shoe business and family life. Anna was a central figure in the family, sharing in the challenges and successes of their immigrant journey. Their partnership was foundational to the family's legacy in the community.

All four of Peter and Anna Adolphson's children attended Gustavus Adolphus College, which is also where I study today. The college has a strong connection to our family history and values. It honors the Adolphson legacy through the Adolphson House, a student residence named after Peter and his relatives. Gustavus Adolphus College represents both education and family tradition in our story.

The Adolphson House at Gustavus Adolphus College is named after my great-great grandfather Peter August Adolphson and his relatives. It serves as a residence for students each year, symbolizing the family's lasting impact on the college and community. This house connects our family's immigrant story to the present day and honors Peter's hard work and dedication. It stands as a tribute to the values he passed down through generations.
